title: Insular half-uncial, the Irish manuscript hand
subtitle: The rounded script of the Book of Kells and related manuscripts
excerpt: Insular half-uncial is the calligraphic hand of the great Irish scriptorium manuscripts, the Book of Kells, the Lindisfarne Gospels, the Book of Durrow. It is a broad-edge script with strongly rounded forms, characteristic horn serifs, and an almost total absence of the sharp angles that define later Gothic scripts.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on manuscript hands: Johnston's analysis of the insular half-uncial forms in the Lindisfarne Gospels is particularly detailed; he used the British Museum manuscript directly as his primary source. The manuscripts themselves are in the public domain by age.

glossaryTerms.definition: A term for the script tradition that developed in the British Isles (Ireland and Anglo-Saxon England) from the 6th to the 9th centuries, distinct from Continental manuscript styles.
A characteristic feature of insular scripts: a small wedge-shaped entry mark at the top of upright strokes, made by pressing the nib into the paper at an angle before pulling the stroke downward.

A minuscule script developed from uncial by reducing the letter height and introducing ascending and descending strokes. In its insular form, the letters are rounder and more compressed than Continental half-uncial.

content.text: is one of the most recognisable scripts in Western calligraphy. The round letterforms and
mark a text as Irish or Northumbrian even to untrained eyes. Edward Johnston called it the most beautiful of all the uncial hands.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (medium)
items.notes: A medium broad-edge nib for the body letters. The insular half-uncial manuscript texts often used a quill trimmed to a moderately broad edge.

items.name: Straight pen holder
items.notes: The insular hand uses a near-flat pen angle, a straight holder keeps the wrist in a natural position.

items.name: Sumi ink
items.notes: Black for maximum contrast. Iron-gall ink is historically appropriate, both work technically.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.notes: Ruled with guide lines: an x-height of 4 nib-widths, with ascenders and descenders at 3 nib-widths each.

content.text: The insular half-uncial is written at a pen angle of approximately 20° from the horizontal, much flatter than Foundational (30°) or Italic (45°). At 20°, the broad edge of the nib produces a very thick horizontal stroke and a near-hairline vertical, the reverse of the Foundational convention. The characteristic roundness of the insular forms comes partly from this low pen angle.

is made by pressing the nib corner into the paper at the start of each upright stroke, then dragging a short diagonal mark downward before swinging into the main body stroke. The result looks like a small wedge or horn projecting from the left of the stroke top. It is not an entry stroke in the Italic sense, the full width of the nib is used, not just the corner.

Four letters set this script apart: the a (two-compartment), the d (upright, not leaning), the g (open descender, a single downstroke), and the r (just a shoulder and descending curve, no vertical). Learn these four first, as they differ most from modern letter shapes.
